data.title: Climb Global Solutions: Market Waiting To See What AI Means (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I first wrote about Climb (CLMB) in the spring of 2024 - that article offers an introduction to the company's business model and corporate strategy. At the time, Climb was experiencing mid-teens organic growth, and management had outlined a strategy - funded by cash - to turbocharge that growth through acquisitions. As I argued then, the market was underestimating the likelihood of that strategy's success. Hence the article's title:

Climb Global Solutions: Cash Can Fund Acquisitions And Double Earnings By 2026

That thesis largely played out over the course of 2025, with shares more than doubling from ~$15 (post-4-1-split) to over $30. Over the last few months, after a recent article where I reiterated my bullish call, shares have given back most of those gains. My October, 2025 article has not aged well, with shares down around 25% since the call.

Two quarters of results have come in and disappointed investors. But since the market is forward-looking, in this article I will focus not so much on the specific results of those two quarters as in what they tell us about Climb's prospects going forward. The most important question for Climb today is how AI will influence (1) demand for the software their vendors sell and (2) their own moat as a software distributor. Climb's margins and billings growth have both disappointed over the last six months, and this has obviously weighed on shares. But the multiples have also compressed significantly as part of the broader SaaS sell-off. Is that sell-off warranted? At the most basic level, it is too early to tell, and that creates uncertainty, which should lead us to discount shares. This article outlines a series of questions to guide investors as we assess results over the next 12 months.

I think shares are probably oversold at current levels, but given the weakening recent results and uncertainty over the future, I'm downgrading shares to a hold (do not initiate). Below, I review recent results based on the Q3 and Q4 earnings PR and call transcripts, which are available from Seeking Alpha.

Q4 Results

Revenue beat in Q4, but adjusted EBITDA and gross profit both declined YOY, and the stock sold off hard. Management said that this was due to quarterly lumpiness - the impact of a single very large transaction in Q4 2024 created a hard comp. VAST Data, one of Climb's largest vendors, had a major transaction in Q4 2024 with an earn-out that was added back into EBITDA, inflating the number in a way that did not reflect underlying organic growth. Stripping out the effect of this one-time charge, according to management, Q4 2025 revenue and billings both grow "in the high teens." But this was bad news. It solidified a trend of gross margin compression over all four quarters of 2025 from about 20% to about 16%. Given that Climb's margins have always been thin, this is a huge problem for the company's profitability. They also suspended the dividend, which drove away some investors who had appreciated the yield. I personally did not care about the dividend, which was very small, and see this as irrelevant to the stock's total return profile, but it matters for the market for shares.

The bigger and more important question is, why the margin compression?

Will AI Competition Hurt Climb's Vendors? Time Will Tell

On the Q3 and Q4 earnings calls, management cited a number of specific factors contributing to the hit to margins. After SolarWinds, one of their largest vendors, was bought out in 2024, the new owners adjusted the pricing model, causing some disruption for Climb. Climb also lost a vendor, Citrix, creating a ~$50m hole. Climb was able to make up for this with other new vendors, but perhaps that came at the expense of margins. More generally, management claimed that the difference in Q4 was an accounting one: they said that more of their product sales under the current vendor mix are being recognized on a gross basis, with the effect of driving up billings faster than gross profit or EBITDA and compressing margins artificially. But this is not a very convincing explanation, in my view, because it doesn't actually explain why EBITDA is flat. The combination of (1) tough comps, (2) accounting changes, and (3) product mix shift together explain the rough quarter, but they don't get the company off the hook. The bottom line is that margins have been under pressure and growth has been decelerating. This is bad news, and begs for a more global explanation.

For that, I think we need to turn to what's happening in the market for software more generally. As you surely know, the conversation over the last few months has been dominated by the idea that AI is eating software. The cost of writing code has plummeted, and the stocks of all sorts of SaaS providers have come under extreme pressure. So, our first real explanation for the stock sell-off is just industry sentiment shift. But more substantively, I think the first concern here is that the price of software is just coming under pressure generally as more companies are able to vibe code their own solutions or buy from just-out-of-the-box AI-powered start-ups. I am not so worried about this particular possibility because many of Climb's large vendors are in the cybersecurity and cloud storage/data management segments, where there are higher barriers to entry.

A second trend that is more immediately concerning is that, in part due to the rise of agentic AI, companies are becoming cheaper with their SaaS budgets and requiring more immediate ROI from their subscriptions. There had been discussion of pressures on enterprise software spending even in 2024, but they have only grown louder in the last 12 months.

The most immediate catalyst for revenue growth will be Climb's partnership with Fortinet, one of the world's largest pure-play security vendors. Climb is currently targeting $250m in Fortinet billings in 18 months, which would represent ~12% growth and thereby potentially increase revenue growth above 20%.

Many of Climb's large and new vendors, such as Darktrace, are "AI-native," and Climb investors might *hope* that they are on the side of the "disruptors" rather than that of the "disrupted." But (1) this is very hard to evaluate, (2) the situation remains quite fluid, and (3) many enterprises are redirecting their software budgets towards their own AI development.

We don't yet know how this will impact Climb. So far, however, the trend is for continued billings growth (indicating that Climb's vendors are competing effectively in this marketplace) with falling gross margins (indicating that, at least for some parts of the market, prices are falling). The bottom line for Climb is lower profitability and heightened uncertainty - bad news.

Will AI Disintermediate Software Vendors? Probably Not

A second-order question is the degree to which AI will enable vendors to circumvent Climb altogether and sell directly to enterprises. The major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) have been pushing their own "marketplace" solutions, effectively entering the market for software distribution.

Despite that possibility, the early signals here have been generally positive. In principle, if AI leads to a proliferation of new kinds of software and new cybersecurity threats, this should make Climb's vendor vetting and brand reputation even more valuable. And there is some early evidence that this is happening. In Q4, Climb signed 2 new vendors from 100 who applied to join the line card - a 100% increase from the average of about 50/quarter in 2023-2025 (see my prior articles for details on this). This suggests that Climb's distribution services are seeing increased demand in an era of AI "eating" software. Competitors TD Synnex (SNX) and Ingram Micro (INGM) have also seen strong revenue growth in recent quarters. While larger marketplaces and in-house software development are definitely risks to watch for, at this point I see relatively little evidence that Climb is at risk of getting disintermediated.

Climb's AI Strategy

In the midst of this discussion about "AI eating software," it's easy to forget that software companies like Climb aren't sitting still. In Q4, Climb announced its own internal AI initiative to improve processes and margins. This "532" plan aims to increase the gross profit margin to 5%, drive SG&A as a percentage of billings from 2.9% to 2.5%, and enable a further 50% in gross billings growth (approximately a 1-2 year runway) with no additional headcount, which would mean approximately an additional 10-20% increase in EBITDA. Climb hired a new CIO from World Wide Technology to spearhead this effort. I doubt that this would be enough to offset the 350 bps drop in gross margin over the last 12 months, but it is definitely a positive sign.

Additionally, Climb's existing limited-line-card strategy positions it fairly well to adapt to disruption in the SaaS space. Since Climb continually reevaluates vendors based on their sales volumes, evaluating new ones and dropping less successful ones, the company should be able to continue benefiting from AI disruption. While the process itself may be bumpy as some big current vendors lose momentum and new ones ramp up, the change itself is not a threat to Climb's basic model. In that spirit, I view the decision to suspend the dividend as positive, offering additional cash for accretive acquisitions that continue to expand Climb's network of customers.

Valuation

Today, Climb trades at 18x TTM GAAP earnings and 15x FWD GAAP earnings, leaving it about 30%-40% cheaper than its industry average, according to SA data. Morningstar gives a slightly lower industry average TTM P/E of 25, but even this would leave Climb 28% below-average. Given that revenue growth has trended in the mid-teens, well above the industry average of 10%, this discount seems somewhat questionable. Climb will, of course, trade at a discount due to its small size, complexity, and low liquidity, but even these don't seem to be enough to account for such a discount. By comparison, larger software distributors like TD Synnex and Ingram Micro trade at around 13-14x earnings, but have slower growth and less attractive unit economics than Climb. Looking at Climb's own history (in the chart below), we can see that it has now reverted back to its longer-term average multiples.

Technically, we can see how those longer-term valuation ranges have served as the bottom of a steep uptrend since 2019, during which time Climb's shares have appreciated at a ~32% CAGR. We might reasonably doubt whether shares can continue appreciating at that pace given revenue growth slowing down to around 10% and margin pressure.

Risks & Bottom Line

In short, Climb has sold off hard on margin compression over the last two quarters. If that deterioration continues, Climb's business will be in jeopardy, as their margins are already quite thin. Tough comps in Q4 made this even worse, with slowing billings growth relative to 2025. It will be important to watch the acceleration of Darktrace and Fortinet revenue over the next several quarters.

But in basic fundamental terms, the future still looks reasonably bright for Climb over the next several years. Vendor demand for their distribution services remains strong, and overall billings continue to grow. The key question going forward will be pricing, which in turn determines gross margins. At this point I think it is too early to tell what will happen in the SaaS marketplace, and so I am staying out of this stock.

data.title: Inflation Reports On Tap
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Fragile ceasefire: JD Vance will head to Pakistan for talks with Iran, while Tehran blocks the Strait of Hormuz as Israel ramps up attacks on Lebanon.

Tensions rise: The Trump administration is weighing a plan to relocate U.S. troops away from NATO countries that opposed the Iran war effort.

Blacklist fight: A federal appeals court refuses to halt the Pentagon's designation of Anthropic (ANTHRO) as a supply chain risk for now.

Data spotlight

Investors will get two major inflation reports this week, with the February PCE Price Index due today and the March Consumer Price Index tomorrow. The core versions of each index will be closely watched to assess underlying inflation trends, as the headline numbers, which include food and energy prices, tend to be more volatile.

What to expect: According to the Cleveland Federal Reserve's Inflation Nowcasting model, February's core PCE is seen rising 2.83% Y/Y, receding from 3.1% in January. Headline PCE is expected to increase 0.4% M/M vs. +0.3% in the prior month, according to consensus estimates. To note, the PCE data isn't all that fresh, as it was delayed due to the government shutdown last fall. March core CPI is estimated to rise 2.60% Y/Y, up from 2.46% in February, Inflation Nowcasting showed. Headline CPI for March is expected to rise 0.9% M/M from 0.3% in February, with the Y/Y pace rising to 3.4% from 2.4% in the prior month, according to consensus estimates.

Inflation outlook: Citi economist Veronica Clark points to several "conflicting and often unrelated factors" affecting core PCE — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — over the coming months. These include higher energy costs, slowing housing inflation, residual measurement issues from the government shutdown, falling equity prices, tariff effects on goods prices, and upward pressure on computer goods from surging AI demand. "After a few volatile months, we expect a clearer picture of slowing underlying inflation to emerge into the middle of the year, allowing Fed officials to resume rate cuts when the labor market data weakens further," Clark said. "But in the near term, market pricing for both cuts and the potential for hikes could be very sensitive to estimates of core PCE inflation."

FOMC minutes: Most Fed officials judged that progress toward the central bank's 2% inflation target could be slower than previously expected due to higher oil prices from the Iran conflict following the effects of tariffs imposed last year, according to the minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee meeting in March. They also saw that "the risk of inflation running persistently above the Committee's objective had increased." Overall, the officials said it's too early to see how the war would affect economic growth or monetary policy. Almost all participants voted to keep the federal funds rate target range at 3.50%-3.75%, with only one dissent. FOMC members did see the potential for rate cuts to resume, but likely later than some of them had originally anticipated.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Top 3 REITs For Reliable Income In Volatile Markets

Launching Main Street Alpha: Focusing On Long-Term Wealth Creation

I Can't Imagine Retiring On Dividends Without These Compounding Machines

Table-Pounding Fat Dividend Picks In This Fearful Market

The Stock Market Rally: Buy Or Fade It?

What else is happening...

Meta's (META) new Muse Spark model seen trailing AI leaders.

Pimco seeks to sell part of $14B debt for Oracle (ORCL) data center.

Visa (V) unveils AI agent-powered payment platform for businesses.

Morgan Stanley (MS) launches ETP tracking bitcoin performance.

Bessent calls on Congress to pass crypto bill amid regulatory gaps.

Treasury to talk private credit ratings with state insurance regulators.

Exxon (XOM) says 6% of its production shut in Q1 due to Iran war.

Valero's (VLO) Port Arthur refinery blast destroyed diesel hydrotreater.

RFK Jr. changes rules to pick CDC vaccine advisors after legal setback.

The long and winding road for GTA VI (TTWO) may finally be ending.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -0.7%. Hong Kong -0.5%. China -0.7%. India -1.2%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.3%. Paris -0.8%. Frankfurt -0.8%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.4%. S&P -0.3%. Nasdaq -0.2%. Crude +5% to $99.08. Gold -0.4% to $4,757.60. Bitcoin -0.1% to $71,369.
Ten-year Treasury Yield -1 bp to 4.29%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Byrna (BYRN) and WD-40 (WDFC).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Innodata: Still Not Chasing Despite Valuation Reset
data.description:
I have been covering Innodata (INOD) since June last year and have not budged from the Hold rating despite a stellar run from June to November 2025. Even in November, I had highlighted the higher-than defendable valuations as the key gating variable preventing a buy. I had also mentioned forward PS levels of ~4x as when things could be more workable. That has been breached, with Innodata correcting almost 33% since then. The purpose of this update is to review whether a below 4x PS valuation automatically qualifies Innodata as a buy. My analysis finds that several factors that were also part of the problem in November still persist - that is, revenue quality still looks uneven and model scalability questions are still unanswered. Hence, despite the valuation reset, I believe Innodata is still a hold. It is a better hold at these levels, but a buy thesis is not compelling enough yet.

Changes Since November

The biggest change since November is the valuation itself. The forward PS ratio was around 8x at that time, down from ~12x levels in the recent past but still looking expensive. That is now below the 4x mark I had indicated as a zone to consider a buy. Even a valuation of ~2.9x based on 2027 revenue expectations still looks higher than the sector median of ~1.8x. So, despite corrections, this is not outright cheap - at best it can be considered fair to reasonable for Innodata's high growth and AI exposure narrative.

In fact, the high-growth narrative has been further challenged since November, with Q4 2025 revenue growing only ~22% YoY, confirming that the slowdown seen in Q3 was not a one-off. Gross margins have also not shown any signs of breaking out above the 40% mark, levels that were broadly intact even in the pre-AI-narrative era.

The correction since November seems to be driven by a broader sell-off across enterprise software stocks. So, this is mostly multiple compression, rather than fundamental deterioration. For Innodata, the fact that I had highlighted in November seems to have been acknowledged now - that Innodata is a second order beneficiary of the AI tailwinds, and does not command platform-level economics. At the end of the day, it is still a human-intensive and services model with little scalability and leverage credentials, as the margins show. The high-growth aspect would have still helped, but that's not hypergrowth level anymore.

Key Concerns Remain

Management commentary in the Q4 2025 earnings call continues to cast doubts around the model's scalability ahead. There was talk around hiring ahead of demand, investments in data engineers and scientists - all pointing to building capacity to support future programs. This is not like a software model where revenue scales ahead of costs - not a new revelation, but it goes to show that the correction happened along with the rest of the software pack but for different reasons. While the enterprise software stocks sold off due to long duration uncertainties and threats from AI commoditizing several aspects of software functionalities, in the case of Innodata, we could be looking at a deeper problem around scalability and operating leverage. Those are not going away, and a rerating could only return if at least the high-growth narrative is upheld.

There was some commentary around automation and systems (across evaluation platforms, agent optimization pipelines, and adversarial simulation systems), but that appears to be addressing complexity rather than reducing headcount dependencies. The new tools and enhancements expand testing, validation, and data generation capabilities, but still require significant human oversight. Improvements in handling higher and more complex workload intensity could be a gain, but do not address operating leverage directly in my view.

For a company with a revenue run rate of ~$280m, $20m of workflows reported as deprecated and replaced by new programs highlights how the revenue is subject to churn and continuous reinvention, rather than steady compounding. The revenue model relies on adapting to new, evolving problem statements and shifting customer requirements that cannot be easily generalized or leveraged across use cases. That is a typical setup in a service-based business.

The reason I stop short of a sell on the basis of the structural limitations of the business, is that there are some legitimate positives that should be acknowledged, in terms of new growth vectors. Innodata is gaining traction across agentic AI workflows, robotics, and physical AI use cases. There are indications of an engagement with Palantir and forays into government or federal opportunities. But the impact cannot yet be quantified to be able to build a high conviction revenue acceleration thesis. Innodata's last couple of quarters at least do not show such traction translating into material growth acceleration - at a time when both Innodata's capabilities and AI demand should have already been at play. The Palantir engagement could be promising, but not necessarily one that impacts revenue sufficiently.

Waiting for Entry

The earlier Hold calls on Innodata were primarily defended through valuations. Now, the primary watchlist items for fresh entries shift to evidence of structural improvement. I will await gross margin improvement trends in Q1 2026 and beyond, toward 45-50%, to be able to say that automation and operating leverage are going hand in hand, at least to an extent. I will, more importantly, await any impact of the traction narrative (including the Palantir and federal inroads factor) on revenue. Over the past couple of quarters, we are seeing ~20% topline growth. Any new traction area that the management highlighted in Q4, like AI safety, should mean that the growth rate should revert toward 30% levels at least. That would tilt the combined revenue growth and FCF margin number decisively above 40. That benchmark is not really applicable to Innodata as a business, but its valuations at ~4x sales are what mid-sized SaaS names command (check Docusign and Twilio, for example).

At the moment, despite the valuation reset, Innodata seems to be still behind expectations in terms of growth and margins. Unless those start to show meaningful inflection from current levels, I still see Innodata as a hold. The positioning in a regime where Innodata can gain from AI tailwinds looks good, and traction appears to be picking up too. But those positives could already be in the price, unless we see a quantified impact of the new engagements discussed in Q4.

data.title: Bed Bath & Beyond Needs To Show How Its Strategy Can Work
data.description:
It's difficult to make a fundamental case for Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) stock at the moment. That's not to say there's no reason to buy the stock at all, but rather that the numbers, for now, don't provide much basis to do so.

To be fair, that's in large part because the numbers are somewhat fuzzy, particularly in the wake of recent deals to bring new brands (and new revenue) into the company. But at the same time, even pro forma for those deals, BB&B appears to be unprofitable on an adjusted EBITDA basis. There is some hope for improvement going forward, certainly, and enterprise value to revenue multiples are somewhat favorable. But those fundamentals in turn are based on the business actually performing better from here.

In other words, the investment case for BBBY centers not on the numbers right now, but rather on how strategy and execution set the company up for the future. And while that strategy, in particular, is intriguing (and innovative), neither seems like nearly enough to bet on the company or the stock at the moment.

The Numbers First

With BB&B bringing on both The Container Store and F9 Brands (including Lumber Liquidators), the current revenue base appears to be near $3 billion annually. In the fourth quarter shareholder letter, chief executive officer Marcus Lemonis said the revenue base, including the legacy Overstock business, Bed Bath & Beyond, buybuy BABY, and Kirkland's, was $1.5 billion. The announced acquisition this week of F9 Brands, whose portfolio is headlined by the formerly public Lumber Liquidators, brings in a little over $500 million in revenue. The Container Store's bankruptcy filing projected 2024 and 2025 revenue in the range of $800 million.

That gets the total to $2.9 billion or so. On a consolidated basis, the business still appears to be unprofitable, even on an adjusted EBITDA basis. BB&B lost $31 million in full-year fiscal 2025. And while Lemonis has said he sees upward of $40 million in savings from integrating the companies (even before the F9 deal), it seems likely that both F9 and The Container Store are adjusted EBITDA negative.

To be sure, that's a guess; The Container Store's bankruptcy filing did project positive adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2025, but the fact that BB&B didn't highlight positive EBITDA for the business may suggest that target wasn't reached. For F9, the existence of an earnout if the division generates just $20 million in adjusted EBITDA in any of the next five years suggests that business, too, remains unprofitable. ($20 million is a sub-4% margin, and five years is a long time.)

These numbers are a bit fuzzy; BB&B no doubt will give more information going forward. But even pro forma for synergies, the consolidated business is maybe at breakeven on an adjusted EBITDA basis. Meanwhile, pro forma market cap at Wednesday's close of $4.82 is right at $500 million. (Including shares at the end of 2025, plus consideration for the acquisitions, total share count seems to be about 104 million.) Net cash is about $90 million (accounting for cash paid and $40 million in financing inherited from the F9 deal), putting enterprise value at $410 million.

That does get EV/revenue to a seemingly attractive 0.14x. On its own, that figure might suggest some upside, given that specialty retailers nearly all get better multiples. But at the same time, Lemonis and BB&B have built a portfolio of brands that have struggled badly over the past decade (that includes Overstock as well), and there's likely some level of near-term cash burn even assuming the consolidated business begins a turnaround.

On the whole, a BBBY bull needs to assume something like 400-600 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. That would get EBITDA (assuming flattish revenue; that may be optimistic) toward the $100-$150 million range, at which point a 6-7x multiple would suggest BBBY stock could double from here.

Buying Retail In The 2020s

But the numbers simply point investors to the qualitative case here. Top-line trends need to improve, and margins need to expand. (In retail, of course, those usually happen concurrently.) And so investing in the new, combined, Bed Bath & Beyond remains a bet on improved execution and the core strategy.

Led by Lemonis, Bed Bath & Beyond has built a portfolio of brands that are intriguing, and acquired cheaply, but simply haven't performed well for some time. (That, of course, also includes the legacy Overstock business.) And in that context, a passage from the Container Store announcement stands out:

Today, the utilization of those assets [TCS has over 100 locations and more than 2.2 million square feet] falls below our expectations. We see a meaningful opportunity to better leverage these locations by expanding assortment, introducing additional brands, and creating a more comprehensive experience for the homeowner.

At its core, this argument is essentially the argument made by executives from (and shareholders in) specialty retailers during the 2010s. In those tellings, the reason why nearly all of those businesses were struggling was not secular change, but internal failings. The Container Store itself is a classic example. Across 2015 and 2016, the company moved aggressively into closets, saw its founder retire, and executed a wage freeze that completely contradicted a multi-decade strategy of "conscious capitalism". The argument at the time was that these massive changes would move the company back to the consistent growth needed to drive margin expansion and a higher share price. Over the next decade, TCS never really hit that bogey (despite some help around the pandemic), and bankruptcy eventually arrived last year.

Kirkland's tried turnarounds. They failed. Pier 1 liquidated in May 2020. Tuesday Morning filed Chapter 11 in 2023. Tile Shop Holdings (TTSH) never really established a turnaround. And the same, broader, issue held in categories like apparel as well. Execution was not the primary problem, nor was assortment. Rather, it was secular pressure on the entire category, driven of course by Amazon (AMZN) but larger, broader trends as well.

The 'Everything Home' Plan

Lemonis's enormously aggressive strategy to create an "Everything Home ecosystem" rests in large part on these businesses getting turned around. They are the first pillar—and, at the moment, essentially the only pillar that actually exists—in a strategy to provide homeowners basically everything they need. Pillar Two will move the company into insurance, warranties, and mortgages. Home services and installation will follow.

But that entire strategy requires the underlying retail businesses to improve. (Some kind of real economic activity in the blockchain businesses tZERO and GrainChain would help as well.) And that in turn requires that Bed Bath & Beyond prove that it can drive growth in businesses that, across the board, have failed to drive growth for essentially a decade now.

And, again, the argument when those struggles began in many cases was that the problems were more self-inflicted than external. Looking back, it is exceptionally difficult (in fact, close to impossible) to believe that argument was correct. It was secular changes, specifically around e-commerce, that undercut the value of brick-and-mortar networks. (For its part, Lumber Liquidators simply wasn't good enough to compete in its industry, but the scale of Home Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW) obviously made its job much harder.)

And so what Lemonis is trying to do is essentially fight a trend that has been clearly established. It's possible, perhaps, to argue that Bed Bath & Beyond can be the exception to the rule. Authentic Brands Group has shown the potential value in bankrupt/distressed brands (though it operates a much simpler and asset-light licensing model, with intellectual property as well). Blockchain efforts might help (though there, too, BB&B has a lot to prove).

Still, at the end of the day, investors in BBBY stock are betting on Lemonis to buck a massive, secular trend. Importantly, they are betting on him to do so with brands that, to be blunt, are faded.

It's possible that Lemonis can pull it off. But at the same time, it is strange to promise, for instance, "the acquisition or development of a scaled residential brokerage network of tens of thousands of agents" at the same time the underlying business is posting declining revenue and orders. There is a sense that Lemonis and his company are trying to sprint before they can walk.

And, at the end of the day, one has to wonder whether betting on Lemonis is the right play, too. The building of Camping World Holdings (CWH) admittedly was impressive (though Lemonis received an assist from famed auto executive Lee Iacocca). But this is simply a different animal, and certainly, judging by the BBBY stock price, Lemonis's involvement to this point hasn't helped. For that to change, Lemonis and Bed Bath & Beyond need to start showing that they can do something that for most retailers—including its new brands—has been impossible.

data.title: Paysign: The Market Is Finally Repricing A Pharma Margin Story
data.description:
Investment thesis

Paysign (PAYS) looks increasingly overlooked by the market. It has been easy to look at the company as simply a niche prepaid card processor or even a payment processor. I believe that framing might now be too narrow, given there was real change within the business, and patient affordability is becoming a higher-margin engine for the company.

The 2025 results support this assertion. Total revenue rose 40.5% to $82 million, with an important nuance. The pharma revenue jumped to $33.9 million from $12.7 million, a huge sign the center of gravity is moving. This is a hint the company is moving to monetize more services per program. This is why I believe the company deserves a closer look.

What changed in 2025

The annual results show a new trend emerging. The gross margin expanded to 59.4% from 55.1%. The management attributed this to a greater contribution coming from the pharma patient affordability business, which carries higher margins than other segments.

This matters more than many anticipate because the offering is more than just co-pay cards. It actually includes pharmacy vouchers and affordability programs designed to serve accumulators and maximizers. The company also argues that its internal platform allows them to speed up adjudications and payments, and customers value that.

A company with a commoditized service proposition will always carry a mediocre multiple, but a company that, through time, is able to administer complex claims while reducing friction, is embedding itself into a high-value administrative layer.

The bull case does not mean we need to ditch the legacy business segment. Plasma still generates $45 million in revenue. This base provides stability and is the organic funding for the next growth branch.

The chart makes sense

The price action is lining up with fundamentals. The company had a sharp rally into 2025, followed by a severe reversal towards the low $3. The latest earnings brought it back to the $5 level. Technically, that looks a lot like a discovery rally, followed by a buying climax, and then a prolonged shakeout before putting on a second attempt at a markup.

The first rally appears to have been a discount, the story movements before everything was lined up. While the recent movements seem like the financials are now validating the story.

Valuation

A look into the valuation metrics positions Paysign well above its peers on the sales multiple. At first glance it could look like Paysign is expensive, but I don't think that is the right conclusion. For instance, Cencora and McKesson are actually giant distribution businesses with big volume and thin margins. They will always look cheap on sales metrics. This actually reinforces our view that the market might have stopped valuing Paysign as a processor/distributor and more like a high-value niche workflow business.

On the other hand, the price-to-cash-flow multiple makes Paysign look cheap. Now, we should take P/FCF multiples with a grain of salt because this ratio on small caps can be noisy, but it is nevertheless a supporting sign, if cash conversion holds.

Risks

The main risk for our thesis is that we might be overestimating the durability of the patient affordability surge. We are assuming fast growth in this segment, but especially, we are assuming it will be a margin engine for the foreseeable future. For it to be true, we need to see program additions staying strong as well as pharma revenue. If this fails, the multiple can compress fast.

Therefore, competition is likely to be the biggest medium-term risk. The company has been extracting good margins, and that will attract competitors, which means they might need to compete on price. This matters because the company's business model is not a perfect moat.

The regulatory complexity also adds an extra risk dimension. For instance, the patient affordability story might lose traction if regulation reduces the economic urgency for accumulators and maximizers.

Finally, I would also highlight the specific stock risk that the market refuses a higher classification for the stock. The bull case hinges on the market changing the perception to a fully healthcare affordability platform. The bear case is the market keeping the perception that the stock is a small, mixed-quality processor. If the market keeps the bear case, the fundamentals might stay strong enough that the stock won't move much.

Bull and Bear scenarios

A good way to gauge this stock is by looking at a bull and bear scenario and seeing where the company is positioned today.

To define the scenarios, we will use the EPS estimates for 2027. For the bear scenario, we will use the low end of the estimates at $0.26, and Haemonetics Corp.'s multiple at 18, which results in a price of $4.68. For the bull scenario, the high end of the EPS estimates is 0.41 and let's use Cencora's 38 multiple, which results in a price of $15.58.

Given the current price of $5.87, these results suggest an annualized return of 63% for the bull case and -11% for the bear case.

Conclusion

Paysign is now starting to be perceived as more than just a prepaid card and plasma payments business. The 2025 results suggest something more, and the market seems open to that. The company has a stable plasma business coupled with a fast-growing patient affordability business that has been the margin engine for the company. That is the whole story for this stock.

The market seems to have picked up on those changes, but it has not necessarily fully priced them yet. The stock is no longer undiscovered, but it still seems caught up between the two contradicting narratives we have exposed in this piece. My view is that the bullish narrative is gaining ground, and as long as the company keeps growing and expanding margins, it will deserve to trade above processor type margins.

Given that so far the fundamentals support the bull story, the company looks like a small-cap rerating story based on financial performance, and our bull and bear scenarios imply a positive risk/reward proposition, I am rating this a buy. Nevertheless, given the business model with an imperfect moat, I suggest keeping a close eye on how the situation progresses.

data.title: What Actually Makes A Muni 'High Yield'?
data.description:
High-yield munis offer tax-exempt income, historically lower default rates than corporate high yield, and distinct sector exposure. Here's what defines this asset class.

What Is a High-Yield Municipal Bond?

A high-yield municipal bond is a tax-exempt debt security issued by a state, county, municipality, or special-purpose entity that carries a credit rating below investment grade, or no credit rating at all. These bonds fund public projects and infrastructure but compensate investors with higher interest payments for taking on additional credit risk.

The muni high-yield market is considerably smaller than its corporate counterpart, with thousands of small, project-specific issuances spread across a fragmented landscape. That complexity also creates opportunities for investors who can navigate it.

What Credit Rating Makes a Municipal Bond “High Yield”?

The standard dividing line is BBB-; anything rated below that, or BB+ and lower, is considered high yield, or “speculative”. In the municipal market, however, definitions can blur. Limited supply of lower-rated bonds has led some participants to include a modest allocation to BBB-rated securities in high-yield benchmarks. We take a stricter view and define high yield munis as those rated BB+ and below, along with unrated securities.

A large share of high-yield munis carry no rating at all. Some issuers forgo ratings because the cost does not justify the issue size, while others simply would not qualify for investment grade. As a result, unrated bonds can represent a significant portion of the market, making independent credit analysis essential.

It’s worth noting that “unrated” does not automatically mean “low quality”. In many cases, a well-known issuer with an established track record will skip the rating process because market participants already understand the credit. The issuer’s bonds trade on reputation and fundamentals rather than a letter grade. In some instances, if these issuers were to pay for a rating, their bonds could very well land in investment-grade territory. For investors, the takeaway is that the unrated portion of the high-yield muni market can contain a wider range of credit quality than the label might suggest.

What Sectors Make Up the High-Yield Muni Market?

Unlike the investment-grade muni universe, which is heavily weighted toward general obligation bonds, the high-yield market is concentrated in project-driven, revenue-backed sectors:

Sector What It Funds
Healthcare / Senior Living Hospitals, nursing facilities, continuing care communities
Education Charter schools, private universities, student housing
Housing Affordable and multifamily housing developments
Industrial Development Manufacturing facilities, economic development projects
Land / Special District New community developments, special assessment districts
Tobacco Settlement revenue bonds backed by the Master Settlement Agreement

Why Are These Sectors More Common in High Yield Munis?

These sectors share a common thread: repayment is tied to a specific project's revenue (patient fees, tuition, rent) rather than a broad government tax base. That project-level dependence introduces additional credit risk, which is why these bonds carry lower ratings or go unrated.

That said, many high-yield munis include structural protections like first mortgage liens, reserve fund requirements, and security covenants that provide layers of protection not always found in similarly rated corporate bonds.

How Does the Tax Exemption Affect High-Yield Muni Returns?

Because interest on most munis is exempt from federal income taxes and potentially state and local taxes, the effective yield for investors in higher brackets can be substantially greater than the stated yield. When adjusted to a taxable equivalent yield, high-yield munis can meaningfully exceed what's available from corporate high yield, even before accounting for munis' historically lower default rates.

Diversification Through High Yield Munis

High-yield munis also offer diversification. The sectors driving this market (healthcare, education, and housing) have very different economic drivers than the consumer cyclical, communications, and energy sectors dominating corporate high yield. That divergence means high-yield munis can behave differently during market stress, potentially reducing overall portfolio volatility.

The VanEck High Yield Muni ETF (HYD) provides broad exposure to this market, tracking the ICE Broad High-Yield Crossover Municipal Index. The index captures the performance of the U.S. dollar-denominated high-yield long-term tax-exempt bond market, with built-in features to enhance credit and liquidity, including an investment-grade allocation that improves tradability. With one of the lowest expense ratios in the peer group, HYD offers a cost-efficient way to access this distinctive corner of fixed income.

data.title: EZU: I'm Sticking With The Fundamentals And Value Too
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the iShares MSCI Eurozone ETF (EZU) as an investment option at its current market price. This fund has an objective to track an index "composed of large and mid-cap equities from developed market countries that use the Euro as their official currency". I started a position in this ETF when 2026 got underway, primarily because it is a more diversified fund than my other European holdings, with 224 companies in its portfolio.

As noted, I was a bull on Europe when the year began and via EZU in particular. This article was posted in early January for reference:

While we have certainly seen our share of ups and downs thus far in 2026, readers may be surprised to learn that the major indices (and EZU) are pretty close to flat year-to-date. However, European shares have beaten out both the S&P 500 and the "world", which means we have gotten some slight "alpha" with this investment option:

In my view it is important to take a measured look at the market right now and try to tune out some of the noise that is rattling investors. Being aware of macro-events like the Iran war can be useful, but it can also distract from fundamentals and cause retail investors to make ill-timed decisions.

For me, I see volatility and panic selling as a time to buy, not sell, but I want to be strategic when I do so. This leads me to EZU, as I continue to see this is a viable option for those wanting developed market, but non-US, exposure. In this light I will use this review to explain why I remain bullish on this ETF.

Europe Has The Value Edge

One of the key reasons I have considered European shares for a while now - and still do - has to do with valuation. While it is not realistic to call the current valuation "cheap", what really matters to me is relativity. Through this lens we can see that the Euro-zone is not as pricey as its developed market peers. While still on the high end of its general trading range, this compares very favorably to both the US and Japan:

As you can see, both American and Japanese large-cap shares are well outside their typical range (in terms of P/E ratios) and are more expensive than Europe in absolute terms. This helps to justify why I want to buy EZU with fresh cash at these levels. The US in particular seems to lack value and I believe European shares are still set-up to benefit from a declining USD - which I expect to occur in the second half of the year. Ultimately, the relative value story is quite clear and it supports keeping my "buy" rating on EZU intact for now.

Europe's Equities Punished By A Rising USD, I Don't See This Lasting

Another factor to consider is how poorly non-US shares have fared as a result of the US/Israel military campaign against Iran. It really shows just how global the world has become in that a conflict Europe is not involved in still rattles their domestic markets. This primarily has to do with Iran's influence over the Strait of Hormuz, with oil's price rocketing higher on any negative headline. While oil is down big as I write this (on 4/8), it is hard to know which direction it is going to go on any given trading day.

The net result has been losses both here and abroad, with European shares dropping more in local currency terms. This is because inflation expectations have limited investor's opinions on rate cuts in 2026, sending the USD higher. As such, the negative currency impact on Euro-dominated shares is clear:

This isn't a pretty sight to be fair. And it extends to EZU in a negative way because France and Germany make-up more than half this ETF's allocation. Given these two countries saw steep drops in the short run, it helps to explain EZU's recent weakness:

So - readers are probably asking - where is the good news? Well, in my view, the worst has probably passed in terms of USD pressure on Euro-shares. I say this for a number of reasons. One, treasury yields (which coincide with USD movements) are near their highest level over a 12-month period. This suggests we are nearing the "top" in terms of how much further appreciation the USD is going to see:

Guessing at a high (or low) is notoriously difficult and I am not going to say yields won't go higher with any certainty. But it does seem to me that yields are likely reaching a ceiling and more upside from here is probably unlikely. This tells me that the next big move for the USD will probably be towards the downside. The impact is that will help non-USD assets appreciate in a reversal of what we have seen over the past 4-6 weeks. That is positive for EZU.

Secondly, President Trump has made it clear he wants lower interest rates. This was a theme in his first term and it has been a rallying cry for him in his second term as well. Yet, some of his actions (such as partaking in the Iran conflict) are pushing prices and inflation higher. This is limiting the Fed's ability to be flexible (meaning cut) interest rates.

But this is not something I see continuing as we approach the mid-terms. I think President Trump is going to ease off on some of these inflationary actions because we have reached a level where consumers are feeling quite a pinch:

What I am driving at here is that I see political realities driving policy decisions out of the White House as we inch closer to the November elections. Metrics like the one above play very poorly with voters and a change of pace is going to be likely. President Trump will start making statements and decisions with inflation in mind, more than he is today, and that is going to drive a weaker USD compared to the Euro in my view. This will set the stage for out-performance for funds like EZU.

The Financials Exposure Is A Tailwind

Another important attribute for why I like EZU in the months ahead has to do with its sector exposure. While this is a diversified fund, it is heavily titled towards the Financials sector. Almost one-quarter of the underlying securities are in this arena:

The primary rationale for my bullish take on this exposure has to do with the uptick in financial activity and deal-making, especially in Europe. This is a global trend to be sure, but if we break it down by region, then Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) recorded the largest year-over-year increase in deal value, compared to other corners of the globe. Notably, there was an 86% increase, supported by several large banking and insurance transactions in Europe during 2025:

This shows me that the slowdown in financial activity we saw from 2021-24 has begun to reverse. The sector entered 2026 with strong wind on its back and I don't think the recent flare-up in the Middle East is going to derail this macro-story over the long run.

Ultimately I see this exposure as a net positive for my portfolio. It helps with diversification in a dual-manner because it gives me non-US exposure (my portfolio is US-centric) and Financials are more dominant here in this index fund than in the S&P 500, so it helps to balance out my large Tech allocation. Given the Financials sector in Europe is helping to lead the globe, this is very important for why I think EZU is a smart play here.

Lack Of Energy Exposure A Potential Pitfall

My final point is one on risk. I laid out what I believe to be a strong case for EZU, and European shares more broadly, going forward. But this isn't a "sure thing". The last month and a half should have hammered that point home!

One central risk, especially in the short-term, is this fund's lack of Energy holdings. The preceding paragraph had a breakdown of EZU's sector weightings and Energy comes in just over 4%. That is pretty minimal and won't offer much in terms of balance if oil prices shoot back up and the Energy sector continues to lead. This is important to keep in mind because the current military conflict could drag on, the Strait of Hormuz could shut back down, and then funds with more Energy or oil exposure are going to out-perform.

This would leave EZU with some likely losses, as we saw how poorly European shares have fared of late. Considering the macro-story has been all about Energy year-to-date, this is something readers should contemplate very carefully before deciding this is an investment they want to make:

The conclusion I draw here is that if geo-political conditions worsen, Energy is likely to keep on leading and that will limit any potential "alpha" from a fund like EZU. While I personally believe the worst shock to oil prices has already occurred, I could certainly be wrong.

I would point out, however, that all countries are not created equally when it comes to how exposed they are to oil price shocks. Even within Europe more broadly, it is the UK household that sees a higher percentage of their resources devoted to energy needs. While countries that make up the bulk of EZU, such as France, Germany, and Spain, are not too far behind, the difference is enough to notice:

The relevance here is that while EZU gives us exposure "across the pond", it lacks British equities since the UK left the Euro-zone. This could come in handy if energy prices do spike, since the British consumer seems to be more exposed. As I noted, I think energy markets will calm down as we approach the summer months, but the Iran situation remains a wildcard. So prepare accordingly going forward.

Bottom-Line

European shares had a bit of a setback of late but have still been out-performing the US on a year-to-date basis. EZU remains a solid choice for this exposure as it offers a Financial allocation that has the wind at its back, it limits Energy exposure which could come in handy if oil prices trend downward (which I expect), and the fund will see appreciation from a weaker USD in the second half of 2026 - which is my personal forecast.

Looking ahead, I think the environment remains ripe for non-USD developed market exposure and this is a fine fund to use to gain that exposure. I therefore am keeping my "buy" rating on EZU in place. I would suggest to my followers they give the idea some thought at this time.

data.title: Gold Volatility Amid Geopolitical Crises: What History Tells Us
data.description:
Gold pulled back amid rising rates and a stronger dollar, but history shows volatility is typical in crises. Strong margins leave miners well positioned if gold stabilizes or moves higher.

Volatility in a Crisis Is Not Unusual

Gold's March performance surprised many investors. Despite a sharp escalation in geopolitical tensions, gold prices pulled back after briefly retesting record highs. That kind of price action may seem counterintuitive, but it is not unusual in periods of crisis.

Gold reached an all-time high of $5,595 per ounce on January 29. Prices pulled back below $5,000 in February but were poised to retest those highs in March as the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran. The attack came on a Saturday, and early the following Monday, gold moved above $5,400. At that point, it looked like gold was on track to fulfill its role as a safe-haven asset.

However, $5,418 marked the monthly high on March 2. What followed was a sharp selloff; gold plummeted $1,319 to a monthly low of $4,099 on March 23 before finishing March at $4,668.06, down $611, or 11.6%, for the month. It appears the bottom may be forming, though volatility remains elevated.

Why Does Gold Fall During Global Uncertainty?

We understand why investors would be disappointed with gold’s performance during a month of global turmoil. Selling pressure overwhelmed safe-haven demand and central bank buying. That said, this type of price action is not unusual when viewed in a historical context.

Gold fell sharply at the onset of the financial crisis in 2008 and again during the early stages of the pandemic in 2020. In both cases, the initial reaction was driven by liquidity needs, rising rates and a stronger U.S. dollar. A similar dynamic was observed after Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. Crude oil rose above $100 per barrel, contributing to higher interest rates and a stronger dollar, and after a short rally, gold declined by roughly 18%.

While each of these periods was shaped by different underlying conditions, they illustrate that gold can experience volatility during the early stages of major global disruptions.

Oil, Rates and the Dollar Remain Key Drivers

The current crisis introduces another oil shock and a new level of geopolitical risk. Higher oil prices have raised inflation concerns and contributed to rising interest rates, a more hawkish Federal Reserve outlook and a stronger U.S. dollar. These forces tend to weigh on gold, particularly in the short term, and can be amplified by systematic and algorithm-driven trading.

At the same time, gold has delivered strong gains since 2024, so some degree of profit taking should not be surprising. Heavy outflows from bullion ETFs suggest that investors are locking in gains or raising liquidity, and gold can often serve as a source of liquidity during periods of broader market stress.

Central banks have been an important driver of gold demand, although activity likely slowed during the recent turmoil. Some countries may prioritize liquidity in times of stress. Turkey, for example, reportedly sold or swapped gold in March to support its currency. Several Gulf States have also been among the largest buyers in recent years, and their activity may fluctuate in the near term.

Once conditions stabilize, central bank demand is likely to normalize. In the meantime, the World Gold Council reports continued buying from countries such as Indonesia, Guatemala and Malaysia, including both new and returning participants. The broader trend of reserve diversification, particularly away from the U.S. dollar, remains intact.

We find it encouraging that the $4,000 level held despite rising rates, a stronger dollar, ETF outflows and uncertainty around central bank activity. Even after the March selloff, gold remains up $349, or 8.0%, year-to-date.

Looking ahead, once the current conflict runs its course, the global backdrop is likely to return to a familiar baseline of uncertainty. The U.S. continues to face elevated deficits and rising debt service costs, while efforts by many countries to reduce reliance on the dollar are ongoing. Higher oil prices also present risks to economic growth. In that context, the longer-term case for gold remains intact.

Gold Miners: Volatility in Prices, Stability in Fundamentals

Gold stocks declined alongside the gold price, with the MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index (MVGDXTR)1 falling 21.4% in March. Even so, the index remains up 5.3% for the year. Despite the volatility, it is largely business as usual for the gold miners.

Are Gold Miners Still Profitable at Current Prices?

At current gold prices, profitability remains strong. Gold in the $4,000 range continues to support growth investment, share buybacks and dividends. Operating margins are robust, with All-in Sustaining Costs (AISC) averaging $1,867 per ounce, according to Scotiabank.

A more complete view comes from Fully Loaded Costs, which include taxes, growth capital, exploration, dividends, interest and general and administrative expenses. Scotiabank estimates these costs at approximately $3,525 per ounce. Taxes and royalties make up the largest portion and are largely outside of company control.

Exploration is one area where companies have flexibility and spending has increased meaningfully. S&P Global reports that mine-site exploration reached a record high in 2025, rising 45%, while overall exploration budgets increased 11%. Whether this translates into production growth in the coming years, it will be an important area to watch.

Higher oil prices are expected to push costs higher, but the impact may be more measured than some expect. Energy exposure varies by operation, with open-pit and remote sites more reliant on diesel, though fuel typically accounts for about 7% of AISC. Estimates from BMO Capital Markets suggest costs could rise 10% to 20% with a doubling in oil prices, but this assumes no hedging.

In practice, many miners hedge fuel costs and maintain inventory, which can delay and reduce the impact of rising prices. For example, Kinross Gold Corp. (4.31% of Strategy net assets as of March 31, 2026) has indicated a sensitivity of approximately $3 per ounce for every $10 per barrel move in oil, when hedging and regional pricing factors are considered.

While no shortages have been observed, prolonged disruptions to key shipping routes such as the Strait of Hormuz could create challenges, particularly in parts of Africa and Asia. However, a significant portion of production is based in the Americas and Australia, where energy supply tends to be more stable.

At this stage, we do not expect material changes to earnings expectations across the gold industry as a result of the recent oil shock. Gold and gold equities have come under pressure, but once the current period of volatility subsides, the same drivers that supported gold above $5,000 remain in place.

data.title: Itaú Unibanco: Execution Still Justifies The Premium
data.description:
The fact that São Paulo–based Itaú Unibanco (ITUB) is a rare case of an incumbent bank in emerging markets that consistently delivers 20%+ ROE across cycles pretty much sums up the investment thesis.

In my last ITUB piece, posted in July of last year, I said that:

Itaú Unibanco's bullish thesis today is supported by: (1) profitability above the average of incumbent peers; (2) diversified loan portfolio; (3) high capital and cost discipline; and (4) high capacity to defend market share.

Unlike domestic peers such as Banco Bradesco (BBD), Banco Santander (Brasil) (BSBR), and Banco do Brasil (BDORY), Itaú sometimes grows at the same or even lower pace, but with better risk-adjusted returns due to better execution of credit growth with selection and a portfolio clean-up.

After posting an FY25 that came in well above expectations in terms of EPS growth, once again reinforcing its track record of outperforming initial guidance.

Entering FY26, against a backdrop of polarized presidential elections in Brazil and with guidance likely to be more conservative, the ~9% YoY EPS growth implied by consensus again seems easily surmountable—and possibly an asymmetric catch for the thesis.

Improving Through a Tough Cycle

First, to provide some context on the Brazilian macro in 2024 and 2025, it is clear that this was an opposite period of "easy money" for domestic incumbent banks.

Interest rates (Selic) remained at ~15% for nearly half of this period, in addition to a still pressured delinquency in certain segments and a more selective credit environment (in the face of high interest rates), contrasted with a scenario of moderate economic growth of 2.3% of GDP in 2025 (deceleration vs. 3.4% in 2024).

Even so, Itaú Unibanco managed to make its ROE go from 22.2% in 2024 to 23.4% in 2025 (or 24.4% on a recurring managerial basis). And this at the same time became more efficient, with its efficiency ratio falling to 38.8%, loan portfolio growing 6% YoY, keeping NPL 90+ at 1.9% (down 0.1 pp YoY), and the cost of credit falling from 2.7% to 2.6%. In short, these metrics show that Itaú managed to grow without deteriorating credit, even with a choppy macro.

The complete delivery of its outlook for 2025 was not necessarily a blowout, as all key metrics were in mid-term guidance. But I believe that here it is worth viewing ITUB under the lens of consistency of delivery and not necessarily in the magnitude of the beat.

More specifically, in about 4Q25, comparing ITUB's key metrics side by side with their domestic incumbent peers, the superiority in the vast majority of them is noticeable. And that's the perfect reflection of a bank that consistently performs better throughout the cycle.

FY26 Guidance Looks Conservative—And That May Be the Point

Itaú provides guidance for 2026, including (1) loan portfolio growth of 5.5% to 9.5% YoY; (2) NII Clients between 5% and 9%; (3) NII Markets between R$2.5 billion and R$5.5 billion; (4) cost of credit between R$38.5 billion and R$43.5 billion; (5) fees and insurance growing 5% to 9%; and (6) expenses ranging from 1.5% to 5.5%.

Although it does not explicitly guide net income and ROE directly, CEO Milton Maluhy Filho made it clear that profitability in 2026 should be "very close to what we observed in 2025".

The remark, also coming from Itaú's CEO, that "we guide conservatively given uncertainties," also leaves an expectation of underwhelming guidance.

Under these expectations, the consensus expects ITUB to grow EPS by 9.3% YoY in FY26, implying an undemanding 9.5x P/E ratio for 2026—compared to the financial sector average of 10x.

Although with a higher profitability base for comparison, which in theory would imply a more arduous path to growth, ITUB's EPS growth projection for 2026E still exceeds the 5.3% YoY and 7.6% EPS projected growth of Bradesco and Santander Brasil, respectively.

In terms of book value, ITUB trades at 2.49x, the highest levels since 2019, when it hit 2.66x, which is not a level that suggests the stock is cheap at all.

But considering that P/B is a function of ROE, the persistent strength in profitability with controlled risks supports a higher fair multiple given its above-average quality.

Therefore, while recognizing that Itaú's multiples have undergone a recent re-rating, I understand that the compounding effect associated with several years of consistent profit growth and high ROE still justifies a constructive and asymmetrical scenario for ITUB.

Lastly, through the lens of the income side of the thesis, Itaú reported a total payout of 72% in FY25, and no explicit guidance was given for FY26. However, assuming ROE remains strong, there is little reason not to believe in a continued capacity for good dividend distribution. So much so that estimates point to a dividend yield potentially at 7.2% in 2026 for ITUB, compared to the current 6.7%—which would be above the average of the last four years of 5.9%.

Key Risks and Considerations

Itaú's management team already signals an explicit expectation of a decline in the Selic, estimating in 2026 a drop to 12.75% from the current 14.75%, showing the base case of easing. A priori, the margin should not collapse, as the loan portfolio and NII growth guidance point to growth, although in my opinion, with excessive conservatism.

That being said, even if the net effect of the fall in interest rates puts pressure on spreads and NIM, the trend is that the improvement in credit, the drop in delinquency, and the improvement in economic activity will counteract it. This is a theoretical scenario, but not guaranteed. My perception is that falling rates will tend to be a tailwind, not a headwind, acting as a rebalancing of drivers. However, this may turn out to be the most sensitive point in terms of bull case risks.

The Bottom Line

Itaú Unibanco has been the big winner among incumbent banks in Brazil for some time, both in terms of profitability, efficiency, and risk-adjusted returns. FY25 showed this dynamic very clearly, and FY26 is heading for something not only similar but marginally better, with a more positive economic cycle, with falling interest rates likely providing an additional tailwind.

The prospect of ROE remaining at elevated levels and a scenario of risks under relative control allows ITUB to trade at demanding multiples of book value, which, in my view, still makes sense given the increasingly demanding expectations for ROE to remain above ~24.5%.

However, it seems to me that ITUB is still priced for a bull case scenario of expansion and long-term ROE growth and needs this trend to be confirmed in FY26 to sustain the rich multiple.

In addition to attractive dividends as a good cushion for EM markets' volatility, I rate ITUB as a Buy and as the best pick among Brazilian incumbent banks.

data.title: SCHD-Inspired 4-Factor Dividend Growth Strategy Selections For April 2026
data.description:
4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio

The 4-Factor Dividend Growth Strategy is an alternative investment strategy to the popular Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Think of it as having your own custom-tailored version of SCHD designed to align with your personal investment style. If you'd like to know more about the initial strategy design and the structure of the first portfolio, you can read about it in this 4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio article.

The strategy was first launched on November 1st, 2022. In a nutshell, the strategy leverages the stock selection process of Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, or rather its underlying index, the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV), with a few minor modifications that tailor the process to align with my growth and quality focus.

Since I prefer a more growth-oriented investment style relative to what SCHD's underlying index offers, each month I build my own custom investable universe to which I apply a modified stock selection process. This is a fully rules-based strategy that runs on autopilot.

My personal portfolio tracking this strategy is rebalanced with newly identified stocks on November 1st of each year. You can read more about the most recent rebalancing in this article.

Stock Selection Strategy

The strategy has a simple objective: to identify potentially high-quality companies that stand out amongst their peers across 4 criteria:

  1. The Free Cash Flow to Total Debt Ratio
  2. The 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate
  3. The Return on Invested Capital
  4. The Forward Dividend Yield

The idea behind these specific criteria is quite simple. I favor highly liquid, low-leveraged companies with a history of high returns on invested capital and strong dividend growth. I prefer to lean towards companies that also sport better-than-average dividend yields. Since each of the 4 metrics is equally weighted, the companies that stand out amongst their peers are generally not the best in any single metric but score well across all 4.

Before we get too far into the details, let's rewind and address what a potentially high-quality company actually is. The 4 criteria do a good job of identifying potentially higher-quality companies, but the process is enhanced by starting with a universe of already potentially high-quality companies.

To accomplish this, each month I run a stock screener that gives me a short list of companies that have characteristics of what I believe to be high-quality companies.

Here are the stock screener criteria:

  1. Exchange: NYSE or NASDAQ
  2. 3-Year Dividend Growth Rate: >5%
  3. 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate: >5%
  4. Payout Ratio - TTM: <80%
  5. 5-Year Revenue Growth Rate: >5%
  6. 5-Year EPS Growth Rate: >5%
  7. Morningstar Moat: Wide or Narrow
  8. Morningstar Stewardship: Exemplary or Standard

Each month roughly 110-130 companies pass all of the filters and make it into the investable universe. From there, I apply the previously mentioned 4-factor ranking process, originally inspired by SCHD. The top 20 ranked stocks are selected for the 4-factor portfolio for that month. These stocks are then held for 12 consecutive months, and the process is repeated.

Track Record

I've been running this strategy on a monthly basis since June 2023, with my actual portfolio launched earlier, on November 1, 2022. I track the lists on a monthly basis to expand my dataset for a more robust analysis. But also to see if any timing plays a role in performance.

Thus far 23 unique 4-factor portfolios have completed a full 12-month cycle, on average generating a total return of 12.62%. My long-term target is 12%, and thus far the strategy has, on average, exceeded this threshold. SCHD's average total return for the same time period is 10.51%, placing my strategy 2.11% ahead. It's worth noting that SCHD has enjoyed strong momentum to start 2026, and it has bridged a significant amount of the gap to the 4 Factor Strategy.

The 12 pending lists, May 2025 through April 2025, that have yet to complete a full 12-month cycle, on average have a total return of 11.68%. For perspective, the average total return for comparable SCHD portfolios is 12.38%, placing my strategy 0.70% behind, on average. Since the returns compound, all of the pending SCHD test portfolios are benefitting from the strong momentum this year, and they were also impacted by the significant sell-off in March.

Looking at the performance results for individual months, here they are compared to SCHD, showing just the portfolios that completed a full 12-month holding cycle:

As you can tell, more often than not, the 4 Factor strategy is delivering alpha relative to SCHD in terms of total return.

Latest Modification

More recently, starting in July 2025, I started testing the inclusion of an expected rate of return in the stock selection process. The objective is to see whether this added filtering metric will yield positive alpha in the long run.

The expected rate of return = Forward Dividend Yield + 3-5 Year EPS Growth Forecast + Return to Fair Value Component.

The return to fair value component is measured by analyzing a 5-year price-to-free cash flow ratio and comparing it to the trailing twelve-month price-to-free cash flow ratio. The potential upside is then annualized over 5 years, based on the reversion to the mean principle.

The expected rate of return is used as a final screening filter applied to the original ranking process. The top 20 stocks with an expected rate of return of at least 12% are included in the portfolio.

Thus far the 10 pending return-based selection portfolios have not fared well against the original strategy, with all tested months except April 2026 leading to underperformance.

This test is still relatively short, and its true merit will be better evaluated after a handful of the portfolios complete their full 12-month cycles. The value rotation in early 2026 has benefitted the original strategy and hurt the more growth-oriented, expected-return-focused selection style.
